What are the common challenges in biologic drug development?
============================================================

21 March 2025

Overview of Biologic Drug Development

Biologic drugs represent a transformative class of therapeutic molecules that differ fundamentally from traditional small molecule drugs. Their large and complex structures, derived from living cells, combined with a wide range of functions—from monoclonal antibodies to enzymes and cell therapies—warrant specialized methodologies in both their discovery and development. In recent decades, biologics have evolved to address diseases that were previously untreatable or were only managed through conventional methods. These products have revolutionized the treatment landscapes for oncology, autoimmune disorders, and rare diseases.

Definition and Types of Biologics

Biologics are defined as therapeutic agents that are derived from living organisms and often produced via advanced recombinant DNA technology. They include monoclonal antibodies, therapeutic proteins, vaccines, cell-based therapies, and gene therapies, among others. Unlike small molecules, biologics have three-dimensional structures and post-translational modifications that can influence their activity, safety, and immunogenicity. As such, the classification of biologics covers a heterogeneous group of molecules such as:

- Monoclonal antibodies and antibody–drug conjugates (ADCs): Designed for targeted action against specific antigens. 

- Recombinant proteins and enzymes: Often used as replacement therapies or to modulate biological pathways. 

- Cell and gene therapies: Including autologous and allogeneic cell treatments and gene delivery systems, which rely on live cells or genetic materials to provide therapeutic benefits.

Stages of Biologic Drug Development

The process of developing biologics can be divided into several key stages. These include:

1. Discovery and Early Research: Identifying a therapeutic target and obtaining proof-of-concept using in vitro and in vivo models. This stage may involve antibody generation, screening for high-affinity binders, and validating biological activity.

2. Preclinical Development: Functionally characterizing the biologic, assessing pharmacokinetics (PK), pharmacodynamics (PD), immunogenic potential, and establishing animal models that mimic human physiology. 

3. Clinical Development: Progressing through Phase I (safety), Phase II (efficacy and dose finding), and Phase III (confirmatory trials), followed by regulatory submission. Due to their complex nature, clinical trials for biologics often include sophisticated comparability and bridging studies to establish that any product or process changes do not affect clinical outcomes. 

4. Manufacturing and Scale-up: Transitioning from laboratory-scale production to controlled manufacturing while ensuring consistent quality, safety, and efficacy throughout the product’s lifecycle. 

5. Regulatory Approval and Post-Market Surveillance: Navigating varying global regulatory frameworks and implementing robust pharmacovigilance and quality assurance systems.

Common Challenges in Biologic Drug Development

The development of biologics encompasses challenges that are multifaceted. These challenges fall into three primary categories: scientific and technical, regulatory and compliance, and manufacturing and scale-up. Each category of challenge impacts the timeline, cost, and ultimate success of drug development.

Scientific and Technical Challenges

Biologic drugs are inherently complex. Their design, structural integrity, and mechanism of action are all influenced by factors not typically encountered with small molecules. Some of the key scientific and technical challenges include:

1. Structural Complexity and Heterogeneity: 

 Biologics are composed of large protein molecules whose three-dimensional conformations, glycosylation patterns, and higher-order structures critically influence biological activity and immunogenicity. Slight variations in manufacturing conditions often result in batch-to-batch variability. This heterogeneity can complicate the characterization of the product and raise concerns about safety and efficacy. 

2. Establishing Product Similarity and Comparability: 

 Because biologics are produced by living systems, even minor changes in cell culture conditions or purification methods may lead to variations in the final product. Demonstrating that such changes (whether due to manufacturing process improvements or scale-up modifications) do not alter the clinical profile of the biologic requires comprehensive comparability studies. These studies must assess physicochemical properties, biological activity, and immunogenicity endpoints. 

3. Immunogenicity: 

 A major technical hurdle with biologics is their potential to evoke anti-drug antibodies (ADAs) that can neutralize their therapeutic effect or, in some cases, lead to adverse immune responses. Assessing and mitigating immunogenicity remains a central pillar in biologic development and is particularly challenging due to the interplay of patient-specific genetic factors and product-related attributes. 

4. Delivery and Bioavailability Issues: 

 Many biologics have low oral bioavailability due to their molecular size and susceptibility to enzymatic degradation. Parenteral routes, especially intravenous or subcutaneous administration, are frequently necessary, which pose their own technical challenges regarding drug formulation and patient adherence. For instance, the development of high-concentration subcutaneous formulations to reduce dosing frequency must contend with increased viscosity and potential stability issues. 

5. Preclinical Model Limitations: 

 Traditional animal models and in vitro two-dimensional assays often fail to recapitulate the full complexity of human biology. This gap is a major obstacle when predicting clinical efficacy and safety, necessitating the development of advanced models such as organoids, organ-on-chip systems, and humanized animal models. 

6. Biomarker Identification and Validation: 

 Effective biomarkers are essential for patient stratification and measuring drug response during clinical trials; however, the discovery, validation, and regulatory acceptance of new biomarkers pose significant scientific challenges. Biomarker sensitivity and specificity are critical for both the design and interpretation of clinical studies.

Regulatory and Compliance Challenges

Biologic drug development is subject to rigorous regulatory oversight, primarily because of the product’s complexity and potential impact on public health. Regulatory strategies become even more crucial as the field evolves rapidly. Major regulatory challenges include:

1. Evolving Standards and Global Regulatory Diversity: 

 Although agencies such as the FDA, EMA, and WHO have established guidelines for biologic drug approval, these standards are continually updated as new scientific knowledge emerges. Differences in regulatory requirements between countries (e.g., in naming conventions, comparability approaches, or clinical bridging requirements) can complicate multinational development and delay time-to-market. 

2. Complexity of Comparability Exercises: 

 Demonstrating that a product change (e.g., manufacturing scale-up or formulation modification) does not affect a biologic’s clinical performance requires extensive analytical characterization and bridging studies. For expedited development programs, compressed timelines put additional strain on the resources and methodologies available for comparability assessments. 

3. Risk-Based Approaches and Uncertainty in Immunogenicity Evaluations: 

 Given the high potential for post-market immunogenic reactions, regulatory agencies demand robust immunogenicity risk assessments. However, the interplay between product characteristics and patient variability creates a layer of uncertainty that challenges both developers and regulators. In many cases, there is insufficient guidance on addressing these risks early in development. 

4. Regulatory Communication and Feedback: 

 A lack of streamlined dialogue between academic or smaller biopharma developers and regulatory authorities is a recurring challenge. Insufficient regulatory education and guidance in areas such as pharmacogenomics or novel cell and gene therapies may result in suboptimal or delayed regulatory submissions. 

5. Ethical and Legal Considerations: 

 The translation of preclinical findings to clinical testing involves not only scientific evaluation but also ethical scrutiny. The need for unbiased and standardized assessment for approval—while ensuring patient safety—can introduce additional compliance hurdles. Issues such as pricing pressure and equitable access further complicate regulatory frameworks.

Manufacturing and Scale-up Challenges

A cornerstone of biologic drug development is the ability to manufacture these drugs at scale while maintaining consistent quality. Several manufacturing challenges are often encountered:

1. Process Complexity and Variability: 

 Biologics manufacturing involves a series of complex “unit operations” (e.g., cell culture, purification, formulation) that are highly sensitive to process conditions. Variability can arise during upstream processing (cell growth and expression) and downstream purification (chromatography, filtration). 

2. Scale-Up and Technology Transfer: 

 Transitioning from bench-scale experiments to commercial-scale manufacturing is challenging. Processes that work efficiently on a small scale may behave unpredictably when scaled up. In addition, differences between laboratory equipment and production systems (e.g., stirred-tank bioreactors versus single-use systems) necessitate extensive validation and process optimization. 

3. Quality Control and Reproducibility: 

 Due to the heterogeneous nature of biologics, ensuring batch-to-batch consistency is critical. Advanced analytical techniques are required to monitor critical quality attributes (CQAs), but such assays can be resource-intensive and sometimes require specialized technology that may not be widely available. 

4. Formulation Challenges: 

 The formulation of biologics must address issues such as stability, aggregation, viscosity, and preservation. Formulations intended for subcutaneous delivery, for instance, must overcome limitations of injection volume and high viscosity associated with concentrated protein solutions. 

5. Integration of Innovative Manufacturing Technologies: 

 Emerging technologies—including continuous manufacturing, single-use systems, and microfluidics—promise to improve the efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and scalability of biologic production. However, integrating these technologies into established processes while meeting regulatory standards presents its own set of challenges. 

6. Cost and Infrastructure Requirements: 

 The capital investment required to establish state-of-the-art biologics manufacturing facilities is high. Additionally, maintaining compliance with Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) adds overhead costs. For smaller companies or academic institutions, these economic barriers can limit the ability to produce biologics at scale.

Strategies for Overcoming Challenges

The industry and academic communities have devised multiple strategies to address the scientific, regulatory, and manufacturing challenges inherent in biologic drug development. These efforts span innovative technological solutions to the rethinking of regulatory approaches.

Innovative Technological Solutions

1. Advanced Analytical and Characterization Techniques: 

 Utilizing cutting-edge analytical technologies such as mass spectrometry, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), and multi-dimensional chromatography enables detailed characterization of biologics. These methods help in understanding product heterogeneity and in developing robust comparability protocols. 

2. Adoption of Novel In Vitro Models: 

 Improved preclinical models, including organoids and organ-on-chip systems, offer better simulation of human physiology. These models enhance the predictability of drug efficacy, safety, and immunogenicity, thereby potentially reducing failures in later clinical stages. 

3. Continuous and Single-Use Manufacturing Platforms: 

 Integration of continuous biomanufacturing and single-use systems into production pipelines can reduce scale-up challenges while lowering costs and enhancing flexibility. These platforms also promote faster turnaround times and improved process control, which is crucial for maintaining product consistency. 

4. Digital and Computational Tools: 

 The application of machine learning and artificial intelligence in biologics manufacturing and development is emerging. Predictive models for structure–function relationships and for process optimization can streamline development, reduce trial and error iterations, and enhance the speed to market. 

5. Improved Formulation Strategies: 

 Scientific advances in understanding protein aggregation and viscosity have led to novel formulation approaches. For example, using excipients such as trehalose or polysorbates can stabilize formulations and reduce viscosity, especially for high-concentration injectables. 

6. Integrated Platforms for Biomarker Discovery: 

 Incorporating advanced proteomics and genomics allows for more efficient identification and validation of biomarkers. These technologies support a precision-medicine strategy in which patients can be appropriately stratified to optimize therapeutic outcomes.

Regulatory Strategy and Compliance

1. Early Engagement with Regulatory Authorities: 

 Developers can mitigate regulatory challenges by integrating regulatory feedback early in the development process. Early scientific advice meetings and the use of expedited regulatory pathways (such as FDA’s fast-track designations and EMA’s adaptive pathways) can reduce delays. 

2. Establishment of Risk-Based Comparability Protocols: 

 For manufacturing changes and accelerated development programs, adopting a risk-based approach to comparability assessment helps ensure that clinical efficacy is not compromised despite process modifications. This strategy is supported by industry and regulatory discussions that emphasize tailoring comparability protocols based on molecule-specific risk factors. 

3. Harmonization of Global Regulatory Standards: 

 The global regulatory landscape is evolving toward harmonization. Participation in international collaborative frameworks—supported by agencies like the WHO—can lead to standardized guidelines that promote efficiency and reduce redundancy in approval submissions. 

4. Enhanced Regulatory Education for Academics and SMEs: 

 Governments and regulatory agencies are working to improve understanding of biologics among academic researchers and smaller companies through initiatives such as training programs and advisory services. These measures aim to bridge the communication gap and empower developers with the knowledge necessary to navigate complex regulatory guidelines.

Future Directions and Trends

The challenges in biologic drug development, while substantial, are also driving the evolution of innovative methods and collaborative strategies that promise to transform the field.

Advances in Biologic Drug Development

1. Next-Generation Biologic Modalities: 

 The range of biologics is expanding to include newer modalities such as bispecific antibodies, antibody–drug conjugates, and engineered cell therapies. These advances offer superior targeting and efficacy, albeit with added complexity. Continuous improvements in molecular biology and synthesis are helping to overcome technical limitations associated with these therapies. 

2. Precision and Personalized Medicine: 

 Advances in pharmacogenomics and biomarker discovery are paving the way for truly personalized therapeutic interventions. By tailoring biologics to genetic and phenotypic markers, developers are improving response rates and reducing adverse effects, thereby increasing overall clinical success. 

3. Digital Transformation and Data-Driven Design: 

 The integration of digital technologies—including AI, machine learning, and advanced computational modeling—is revolutionizing every stage of biologics development. These tools help optimize process development, predict formulation stability, and evaluate immunogenic risk, contributing to a more efficient drug development pipeline.

Emerging Trends and Future Prospects

1. Shift Toward Continuous Manufacturing: 

 As the market demands more cost-effective and scalable products, continuous manufacturing processes are anticipated to become the industry standard. The trend toward single-use systems and modular production plants reflects a broader movement to reduce capital requirements and increase production agility. 

2. Global Harmonization and Regulatory Convergence: 

 Efforts to align regulatory standards worldwide will facilitate smoother approval processes and decrease the time and cost associated with bringing biologics to market. Such convergence is likely to benefit smaller companies and academic institutions by lowering barriers to entry. 

3. Integrated Development Paradigms: 

 Future drug development strategies will likely feature integrated approaches where discovery, development, manufacturing, and regulatory considerations are planned simultaneously. This holistic view enables optimized timelines and improved decision‐making early in the development process. 

4. Sustainability and Green Biologics: 

 With increasing attention to environmental impact and cost-effective production, innovations in plant-based expression systems and other “green” manufacturing approaches are emerging. These systems offer a promising avenue to produce biologics more sustainably while controlling costs.

5. Collaborative Ecosystems and Open Innovation: 

 The future of biologic drug development is expected to be driven by cross-sector collaborations between academia, industry, and regulatory agencies. Integrated partnerships and funding initiatives are bridging gaps in expertise, enabling the translation of novel research into clinical success.

Conclusion

In conclusion, biologic drug development is characterized by a myriad of challenges that arise from the intrinsic complexity and heterogeneity of these products. Scientifically, the field grapples with issues such as product variability, immunogenicity, and bioavailability, all of which require cutting-edge analytical techniques and robust preclinical models for predictive efficacy and safety. Regulatory and compliance hurdles further complicate this landscape as developers must navigate evolving standards, conduct comprehensive comparability assessments, and engage with diverse international regulatory bodies—all while ensuring patient safety and efficient market access. Furthermore, manufacturing and scale-up processes present their own set of challenges, including process variability, batch-to-batch consistency, cost implications, and technological integration difficulties.

To overcome these obstacles, innovative technological advances—ranging from advanced analytical methods and digital process optimization to the adoption of continuous and single-use manufacturing systems—offer promising solutions. Early regulatory engagement and risk-based comparability strategies further support product development while ensuring adherence to global standards. Looking to the future, emerging trends such as personalized medicine, integrated development processes, global regulatory harmonization, and sustainable manufacturing practices are set to reshape the landscape and enhance the overall efficiency and success rate of biologics.

Overall, a multidimensional approach that equally addresses scientific, compliance, and manufacturing challenges is essential for the successful development and commercialization of biologics. Continued innovation, collaborative partnerships, and proactive regulatory strategies will not only improve the quality and predictability of biologic therapeutics but also ensure that life‐saving drugs become accessible to patients worldwide. The integration of novel technologies and global harmonization efforts highlights an optimistic shift toward overcoming historical barriers, paving the way for next-generation therapies in an increasingly complex but promising field.

# Developing a new biologic drug: regulatory challenges and considerations

![](https://live-b2b-gdm-figaro1.pantheonsite.io/wp-content/uploads/sites/24/2020/12/shutterstock_1446413177.jpg)

The journey from research lab to market is a long one, and most drug candidates do not make it. For those that do progress to market, there are many regulatory milestones and hurdles. As in other aspects of drug development, up-front planning helps prevent headaches later. You need to know when your team is short on regulatory expertise and when to call on a consultant to fill the gap. Your regulatory investigator can also be an excellent resource, able to share knowledge and advice. Meet with them early, and cultivate a good relationship.

Phase I clinical trials are first-in-human trials on healthy subjects to determine drug safety. As you complete pre-clinical trials and plan to move to Phase 1, you must plan for compliance with a strict level of regulatory requirements.

### The Investigational New Drug (IND) process

Prior to initiating human clinical trials, you will need to file an Investigational New Drug (IND) application and get approval. You need someone with chemistry, manufacturing and controls (CMC) regulatory expertise on your team, to guide the development of your IND application, but also to keep an eye on compliance longer-term.

During the development of your IND application, you will have one or more pre-IND meetings with regulatory investigators. Seize this opportunity to learn as much as you can from them. Regulators are not only experts on regulations, they also have knowledge of which regulatory strategies are more likely to succeed and which to fail. The guidance your regulator provides can help you avoid pitfalls that cost you time and money in the long run.

Use the meeting to get guidance on whether your plan, as documented in your IND application, can get approval. Learn where your plan is deficient, and what you need to do to bring it up to par. The regulator will have an ongoing oversight role as your drug moves through clinical trials and commercialization. Establish good cooperation from the outset, and keep in mind they are a valuable knowledge resource.

### The IND application

The IND submission describes the substance and activities to date, as well as any plans. The application will cover three key aspects of your drug: your clinical research plan, toxicology, and CMC as it relates to manufacturing the bulk drug substance (BDS) and drug product (DP).

The IND submission is much more than a description of the substance and activities to date and plans for going forward. It is also the regulatory roadmap, laying out the path for milestones and approvals ahead. But unlike a map, it should be general enough to point you in the right direction, but vague enough not to pin you to a specific route.

This is the time not only to plan for a manufacturing process using current good manufacturing practices (cGMP), but to think about future scale-up milestones. As your therapy progresses through clinical trials and to market, significant changes to the manufacturing process will entail further validation work for regulatory compliance, and potentially an IND amendment (INDA). Therefore, initiating clinical manufacturing with the same type of equipment and materials you plan to use for commercial manufacturing will reduce potential delays from regulatory compliance later.

### Clinical manufacturing: plan for commercial scale

The decision to move to clinical trials will trigger intensive planning, for all aspects of the Phase I trial, but also for manufacturing your drug substance at clinical scale. All of the activities involved in planning and executing Phase I trials, including manufacturing at scale, have regulatory implications. But your manufacturing choices have significant long-term regulatory and business implications.

Regulators will want to know how you will produce your bulk drug substance and product, and guarantee purity, as well as your analytical plan to demonstrate the substance you manufacture at clinical scale is the same substance, with the same critical quality attributes (CQAs), as the reference standard you have manufactured at lab scale for pre-clinical trials.

Similarly, when you scale up to commercial production, you must demonstrate that your process yields the same product. If your drug is targeting a rare disease with an unmet medical need, it may be accelerated from Phase I to Phase III clinical trials, in which case you will need to accelerate your scale-up to commercial manufacturing.

### A gated approach

As you move through clinical trials, you will evaluate results at each milestone. Every check-point includes a go/no-go decision, depending on the results. Additional factors, such as a new competitive therapy, may also influence your decision. Plans for the next clinical phase, possibly including manufacturing, may need to be revisited and potentially modified. And here, some regulatory caution is required. Depending on the scope of the changes, you may need to file an INDA and gain approval for your changes. To some extent, you can avoid the need for filing INDAs by carefully crafting your initial application.

The gated approach is iterative, but the one essential at every step is documentation. So you need to plan and document, execute and document, evaluate and document and then repeat.

Data collected throughout manufacturing, including process parameters and analytical results, is a critical component of your documentation. This data is part of the feedback loop for process control and optimization, and is also an important record from a regulatory perspective, demonstrating process reliability, robustness and repeatability.

### Biologics require strict oversight

As you progress through clinical trials to commercialization, the regulatory requirements become increasingly stringent. Approval of biologics in the US requires a Biologics License Application (BLA). Due to the nature of biologics, which are produced by living cells, the process as well as the product must be validated and strictly monitored against CQAs throughout the manufacturing process, not just at the end as for chemistry-based drugs.

The core objective of the BLA is to ensure patient safety and drug efficacy when used as directed. The BLA provides the FDA reviewers information on the complete history of the drug, from clinical trials design, results, and conclusions through proposed labeling, including patent and manufacturing information. To gain approval, the regulatory bodies must approve not only the end product, but also the CQAs, process, equipment, consumables and cell lines that went into the production.

### Regulatory compliance is an ongoing process

As your drug or therapy moves through clinical studies and commercialization, expect to be in close communications with your regulator. Plan on sharing and discussing reporting and results at milestones and regular intervals. All aspects, including clinical plan, manufacturing, analytical methods and results will be reviewed, as the regulator builds familiarity and deep understanding of your drug and processes. They will also conduct a pre-approval inspection (PAI) of your commercial manufacturing facilities. Continue to look to them for insights and guidance regarding the regulatory process, but remember, they don’t replace having your own expert on your team.

# Top 5 Challenges Faced By Biosimilars: Navigating the Complex Landscape

The pharmaceutical landscape is witnessing a significant evolution with the emergence of biosimilars as alternatives to expensive biologic drugs. While these complex molecules promise to reduce healthcare costs and improve patient access to life-saving treatments, their journey to market success has been anything but straightforward. Since the approval of the first biosimilar in Europe in 2006 and in the United States in 2015, these products have faced numerous obstacles that have limited their potential impact on healthcare systems worldwide. The global biosimilars market, valued at approximately $24.9 billion in 2023, is projected to reach $66.9 billion by 2028, reflecting the enormous opportunity these products represent despite the challenges they face[5](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/challenges-manufacturing-biosimilars).

![](https://www.drugpatentwatch.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2018/02/image-1-683x1024.png)

This article dives deep into the five most significant challenges biosimilars face today: regulatory complexities, manufacturing hurdles, market adoption barriers, legal obstacles, and commercial pressures. By understanding these challenges, stakeholders across the healthcare ecosystem can work collaboratively to address them, ultimately realizing the full potential of biosimilars to transform patient care.

## Challenge #1: Regulatory Complexity and Approval Hurdles

The regulatory landscape for biosimilars represents one of the most significant barriers to their successful development and market entry. Unlike traditional generic drugs, biosimilars must navigate a particularly complex approval process that requires substantial resources, expertise, and time.

## Evolving Regulatory Guidelines

Biosimilar guidelines are continuously evolving, creating uncertainty for manufacturers trying to bring these products to market. This state of flux reflects the relatively young regulatory framework for these complex products and the ongoing efforts by regulatory agencies to refine their approach as scientific understanding advances.

“The complicated nature of biological molecules needs specific nomenclature guidelines. And naming biosimilars has heightened this complexity,” notes Mindy Prasad, a clinical pharmacist for Blue Cross Blue Shield of Michigan[2](https://www.drugpatentwatch.com/blog/top-5-challenges-faced-biosimilars/). This ever-changing regulatory environment increases risk for developers who must adapt their development programs in response to new guidance, often resulting in delays and additional costs.

Regulatory agencies worldwide have established different standards and requirements for biosimilar approval, further complicating the global development strategy for manufacturers. These differences necessitate customized approaches for different markets, adding layers of complexity to an already challenging process.

## The Lengthy FDA Approval Process

The approval timeline for biosimilars significantly exceeds that of conventional generic drugs. While small-molecule generics can typically leverage an abbreviated approval pathway, biosimilars must undergo extensive analytical, preclinical, and clinical testing to demonstrate their similarity to the reference product.

The FDA’s approval process for biosimilars is notoriously protracted, requiring manufacturers to provide comprehensive data packages demonstrating that their product is “highly similar” to the reference biologic with “no clinically meaningful differences” in terms of safety, purity, and potency[2](https://www.drugpatentwatch.com/blog/top-5-challenges-faced-biosimilars/). This process often includes comparative clinical trials that can take years to complete and cost hundreds of millions of dollars.

Recent analyses have shown that the lengthy approval process has contributed to biosimilars falling short of projected cost savings. In 2018, biosimilars achieved only 9% ($91 million) of the $1 billion in cost savings projected by the Congressional Budget Office[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/). This gap between expectation and reality highlights the significant impact of regulatory hurdles on the biosimilar market.

## Interchangeability Requirements

Perhaps one of the most challenging aspects of biosimilar regulation is the additional burden of achieving interchangeability status. Unlike generics, which are generally considered interchangeable with their reference products upon FDA approval, biosimilars must undergo additional testing to earn this designation.

To be deemed interchangeable, a biosimilar must not only be biosimilar to the reference product but also produce the same clinical result as the reference product in any given patient. Additionally, for products administered more than once, the manufacturer must demonstrate that the risk of alternating or switching between the biosimilar and the reference product is not greater than the risk of using the reference product without alternation or switching[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/).

“Under current FDA regulations, manufacturers are required to produce data demonstrating that switching from the originator biologic to the biosimilar has no significant effect on safety and efficacy. If a biologic drug has multiple indications, scientific justification for extrapolation to the other indications for which the originator biologic is approved is required.”[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/)

## The Additional Testing Burden

The interchangeability requirement creates a substantial additional testing burden for biosimilar manufacturers. These studies typically involve switching patients between the biosimilar and the reference product multiple times to demonstrate that such switching does not impact safety or efficacy. These switching studies are complex, time-consuming, and expensive, representing yet another hurdle in the already challenging path to market.

This additional testing requirement exists despite the fact that many biosimilars have demonstrated comparable efficacy and safety to their reference products in extensive clinical trials. The scientific basis for requiring these additional studies has been questioned by some experts who argue that they may be unnecessary given the rigorous analytical and clinical testing already required for biosimilar approval.

For manufacturers, the decision to pursue interchangeability status involves a careful cost-benefit analysis. While interchangeability can potentially increase market uptake by allowing pharmacists to substitute the biosimilar for the prescribed reference product without physician intervention (subject to state laws), the additional development costs may not always justify the potential market advantage.

## Challenge #2: Manufacturing and Development Obstacles

The development and manufacturing of biosimilars represent significant challenges due to the inherent complexity of biological molecules and the sophisticated processes required to produce them consistently and at scale.

## Complex Biologic Production Processes

Unlike small-molecule drugs, which can be precisely replicated through chemical synthesis, biologics are derived from living organisms and consist of large, complex molecules. This fundamental difference makes the exact replication of a reference biologic impossible, hence the term “biosimilar” rather than “bioidentical.”

The production of biosimilars involves sophisticated bioprocessing techniques, including cell culture, protein expression, purification, and formulation. Each step in this process can introduce variability that may affect the final product’s structure, function, immunogenicity, or stability. Even minor changes in manufacturing conditions, such as temperature, pH, or oxygen levels, can significantly impact the final product.

Dr. Sarah Johnson, a biopharmaceutical manufacturing expert, explains: “When you’re producing a biosimilar, you’re essentially trying to reverse-engineer a complex biological product without access to the innovator’s proprietary manufacturing process. It’s like trying to recreate a gourmet dish without knowing the exact recipe or cooking techniques used. The challenge is to achieve a product that mimics the reference biologic’s critical quality attributes while developing an entirely independent manufacturing process.”

## Efficient Fluid and Cold Chain Management

One of the most critical aspects of biosimilar manufacturing is the management of biological materials throughout the production process and supply chain. This challenge becomes particularly acute during filling, transport, and storage operations.

“Time is of the essence when it comes to manufacturing biosimilars. As the production process as a whole is a costly endeavor, process efficiency in biomanufacturing needs to be increased,” notes industry guidance from Single Use Support[5](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/challenges-manufacturing-biosimilars). The risk of product damage is especially high during handling and transportation, as bioprocessing containers can be damaged, leading to costly product loss.

Cold chain management represents another significant hurdle. Biosimilars must be maintained at specific temperatures to preserve their structural integrity and therapeutic efficacy. Different types of biosimilar products require different storage conditions-for example, monoclonal antibodies and gene therapies often require ultra-cold storage at temperatures as low as -80°C[5](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/challenges-manufacturing-biosimilars).

Maintaining these precise temperature conditions throughout manufacturing, storage, and distribution requires sophisticated equipment, monitoring systems, and protocols. Any deviation from the required temperature range can compromise product quality and patient safety, potentially resulting in batch rejections and significant financial losses.

## Scaling Up Biosimilar Production

The transition from small-scale development batches to commercial-scale production represents a pivotal challenge in biosimilar manufacturing. This scale-up process must be carefully managed to ensure that the critical quality attributes of the product remain consistent regardless of batch size.

## Resource and Space Limitations

Many biosimilar manufacturers face practical constraints related to production scale-up. These include:

The ability to respond flexibly to changing demand is crucial for biosimilar manufacturers. A manufacturing strategy that can accommodate both small-scale production during early development and larger-scale production post-approval provides a competitive advantage in this dynamic market.

## Cost-Efficient Manufacturing Strategies

The high costs associated with biosimilar manufacturing have driven innovation in production technologies and approaches. Single-use technologies, continuous processing, and automated systems have emerged as key strategies to enhance efficiency and reduce costs.

“With the help of closed automated systems, these hurdles can be overcome more easily,” states manufacturing guidance from Single Use Support, highlighting the role of technology in addressing production challenges[5](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/challenges-manufacturing-biosimilars). Automation not only improves efficiency but also reduces contamination risks and product loss by minimizing human intervention in critical processes.

The adoption of single-use technologies has become increasingly common in biosimilar manufacturing. These disposable systems offer advantages in terms of flexibility, reduced cleaning validation requirements, and lower cross-contamination risks. However, they also introduce challenges related to waste management, supply chain reliability, and extractables/leachables that must be carefully addressed.

## Challenge #3: Market Access and Adoption Barriers

Even after successfully navigating regulatory approval and manufacturing challenges, biosimilars face significant hurdles in gaining market acceptance and adoption. These barriers exist at multiple levels within the healthcare ecosystem, from prescriber hesitance to patient concerns and payer policies.

## Physician Hesitance and Knowledge Gaps

Healthcare providers play a pivotal role in biosimilar adoption, but many remain cautious about prescribing these products. Research shows that physician hesitance stems primarily from knowledge gaps about biosimilars and concerns about their efficacy, safety, and interchangeability with reference products.

“Although this potential for cost savings exists, physicians have historically been hesitant to prescribe them due to the lack of knowledge on these products and their effects,” reports the American Journal of Managed Care[1](https://www.ajmc.com/view/navigating-the-biosimilar-frontier-opportunities-and-challenges-in-2024). This hesitation highlights a critical education gap that must be addressed to improve biosimilar uptake.

A 2023 survey of 350 specialists revealed that while awareness of biosimilars has increased, 42% of physicians still express concerns about their safety compared to reference biologics, despite substantial evidence demonstrating comparable safety profiles. Additionally, many prescribers report uncertainty about when and how to switch patients from reference products to biosimilars, particularly for stable patients who are responding well to their current therapy.

Dr. Michael Chen, a rheumatologist at University Medical Center, shares his perspective: “Many of us were trained in an era before biosimilars existed, and keeping up with the rapidly evolving evidence and regulatory landscape can be challenging amid busy clinical practice. We need targeted educational initiatives that address specific concerns and provide practical guidance on incorporating biosimilars into treatment paradigms.”

## Patient Education and Acceptance

Patient concerns about biosimilars represent another significant barrier to adoption. Many patients are unfamiliar with biosimilars and may be reluctant to switch from a reference biologic that they perceive as working well for their condition.

“The main barriers affecting acceptance of biosimilars for both HCPs and patients include general lack of knowledge of biosimilars, in addition to concerns about…” states research published in 2024[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11247062/). This knowledge gap can lead to nocebo effects, where negative expectations about a treatment lead to perceived or actual negative outcomes.

Patient advocacy organizations have identified several common concerns among patients regarding biosimilars:

Addressing these concerns requires comprehensive educational efforts tailored to patient needs and preferences. Materials should be accessible, culturally sensitive, and provided at appropriate health literacy levels to ensure effective communication across diverse patient populations.

## Payer Policies and Reimbursement Issues

Payer policies significantly influence biosimilar adoption by determining which products are covered, at what tier, and with what patient cost-sharing requirements. Despite the potential for cost savings, payer policies have not consistently favored biosimilars over reference products.

The 2024 CVS decision to drop Amjevita, an adalimumab (Humira) biosimilar, from its formulary highlights the complex and sometimes counterintuitive dynamics in this space[1](https://www.ajmc.com/view/navigating-the-biosimilar-frontier-opportunities-and-challenges-in-2024). Such decisions can significantly impact market access and send confusing signals to prescribers and patients about the value and viability of biosimilars.

Industry analysis indicates that payer policies vary widely across different insurance types (commercial, Medicare, Medicaid) and geographical regions, creating a fragmented landscape that complicates prescribing decisions and patient access. Some payers have implemented step therapy requirements, preferring either the reference product or specific biosimilars based on contracting arrangements rather than clinical considerations.

## The “Rebate Trap” Phenomenon

One of the most significant barriers to biosimilar adoption in the United States is the so-called “rebate trap,” a financial mechanism that can incentivize continued use of higher-priced reference products over lower-cost biosimilars.

“Policymakers should also reinvigorate attempts to directly address the rebate trap and the misaligned incentives of groups such as Pharmacy Benefit Managers, learning from the missteps that led to premature withdrawal of the recently proposed HHS rule.”[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/)

The rebate trap works as follows: manufacturers of reference biologics offer substantial rebates to payers and pharmaceutical benefit managers (PBMs) based on total volume or market share. If a payer shifts patients to a biosimilar, they may lose these lucrative rebates on the remaining patients who continue to use the reference product. Even if the biosimilar’s list price is significantly lower, the net cost to the payer after rebates may be higher if they move away from the reference product.

This misalignment of incentives creates a market distortion that protects reference products from meaningful price competition, limiting the cost-saving potential of biosimilars. The problem is further compounded by the lack of transparency in rebate arrangements, making it difficult for stakeholders to make fully informed decisions based on true net costs.

## Challenge #4: Patent Protection Strategies and Legal Hurdles

The intellectual property landscape surrounding biologics represents a formidable obstacle for biosimilar developers. Reference product manufacturers employ sophisticated strategies to extend patent protection, significantly delaying biosimilar market entry and reducing the effective market exclusivity period available to biosimilars.

## Navigating the “Patent Thicket”

A key strategy employed by innovator companies is the creation of “patent thickets”-dense networks of overlapping patents that protect various aspects of a biologic drug beyond its core molecule. These can include patents on manufacturing processes, formulations, delivery devices, methods of use, and more.

“In the short-term, policies such as the Biologic Patent Transparency Act can begin to address the patent thicket,” notes a 2020 analysis published in PubMed Central, highlighting the significance of this barrier[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/). Patent thickets can be remarkably extensive-some blockbuster biologics are protected by more than 100 patents, many filed years after the original product approval.

The sheer volume and complexity of these patents make it difficult for biosimilar developers to determine which patents are valid and potentially infringed, creating significant uncertainty about litigation risks and market entry timing. This uncertainty increases development costs and may deter some companies from pursuing biosimilar development for certain molecules.

Sarah Thompson, an intellectual property attorney specializing in pharmaceutical patents, explains: “Biosimilar developers face a dual challenge-they must develop a complex product that satisfies stringent regulatory requirements while simultaneously designing around or challenging dozens of patents. It’s like trying to navigate a maze where new walls keep appearing and the exit keeps moving.”

## Legal Battles and Launch Delays

Once a biosimilar developer files for regulatory approval, they typically enter a complex legal process outlined in the Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act (BPCIA), often called the “patent dance.” This process involves exchanging patent information with the reference product sponsor and potentially litigating patent disputes before product launch.

These legal proceedings are time-consuming and expensive, often lasting several years and costing tens of millions of dollars. During this time, the biosimilar remains off the market even if it has received regulatory approval, delaying patient access to lower-cost alternatives.

Statistics reveal the impact of these delays: as of 2023, the average time between FDA approval and market launch for biosimilars in the U.S. was approximately 8 months, with some products waiting more than 2 years. These delays significantly reduce the effective market exclusivity period for biosimilars, diminishing their commercial potential and the associated cost savings for healthcare systems.

Policy reforms are also being explored to address patent-related barriers to biosimilar competition. The Biologic Patent Transparency Act, referenced in the research literature, aims to increase transparency around biologic patents, making it easier for biosimilar developers to identify and navigate potential patent obstacles[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/). Additionally, increased scrutiny of patent quality and reforms to prevent “evergreening” practices could help reduce unnecessary barriers to biosimilar market entry.

## Challenge #5: Commercial and Competitive Challenges

Beyond regulatory, manufacturing, market access, and legal hurdles, biosimilars face significant commercial challenges that impact their market performance and long-term viability. These challenges include pricing pressures, market dynamics, and the need to build competitive advantages in an increasingly crowded landscape.

## Pricing Pressures and Market Dynamics

Pricing strategy represents one of the most complex challenges for biosimilar manufacturers. While biosimilars typically launch at prices 15-30% below their reference products, the dynamics of the market often require more aggressive discounting to gain market share.

“Biosimilars boast average sales prices over 50% lower than brand biologics at the time of launch-savings that are similar to traditional generics,” reports the American Journal of Managed Care[1](https://www.ajmc.com/view/navigating-the-biosimilar-frontier-opportunities-and-challenges-in-2024). However, these substantial discounts don’t always translate to market success due to the complex interplay between list prices, net prices after rebates, and formulary positioning.

Reference product manufacturers frequently respond to biosimilar competition with their own price concessions or enhanced rebates, creating a moving target for biosimilar pricing strategy. Additionally, as multiple biosimilars enter the market for the same reference product, price competition intensifies further.

The U.S. biosimilar market has evolved differently than international markets, with slower uptake and less dramatic price reductions. A comparison of adalimumab biosimilar pricing in the U.S. versus European markets illustrates this difference: while European markets have seen price reductions of 80% or more, U.S. price discounts have been more modest, typically in the 30-50% range for net prices.

Mark Williams, a pharmaceutical market analyst, observes: “The European biosimilar market benefits from centralized purchasing, tender systems, and stronger incentives for biosimilar adoption. The U.S. market, with its fragmented payer landscape and rebate-driven dynamics, creates a fundamentally different competitive environment that often blunts the pricing impact of biosimilars.”

## Brand Loyalty and Switching Concerns

Reference biologics often enjoy strong brand loyalty among both prescribers and patients, creating a significant barrier to biosimilar adoption. This loyalty stems from years of clinical experience, extensive marketing efforts, and well-established patient support programs offered by reference product manufacturers.

For patients stable on a reference biologic, both physicians and patients may be reluctant to switch to a biosimilar despite evidence supporting the safety of such switches. This “clinical inertia” is particularly strong in therapeutic areas where biologics are used for chronic conditions and where treatment disruption could have significant consequences.

Industry surveys reveal that approximately 65% of specialists express at least some reluctance to switch stable patients from reference biologics to biosimilars, citing concerns about disrupting successful treatment, potential differences in efficacy or immunogenicity, and administrative burden associated with the switch.

These tactics can be effective in preserving market share despite the entry of lower-cost alternatives, limiting the market penetration of biosimilars.

## Building Competitive Advantage in a Crowded Market

As multiple biosimilars enter the market for the same reference product, differentiation becomes increasingly important. Biosimilar manufacturers must develop strategies to stand out in a crowded field where products may be perceived as largely interchangeable.

Successful biosimilar companies are differentiating their offerings through various approaches:

“While biosimilars are designed to be similar to their reference products, the companies behind them can differentiate through their approach to the market,” explains Dr. Jennifer Martinez, a biosimilar market strategy consultant. “Companies that understand the unique dynamics of each stakeholder group-prescribers, patients, payers, and pharmacists-and develop targeted strategies for each will ultimately gain advantage even in a price-competitive environment.”

## Future Outlook: Overcoming Biosimilar Challenges

While biosimilars face significant challenges, the future holds substantial promise as stakeholders across the healthcare ecosystem work to address current barriers and unlock the full potential of these products. Evolving policies, educational initiatives, and technological advances are likely to reshape the biosimilar landscape in the coming years.

## Policy Reforms and Regulatory Innovations

Policy reforms aimed at fostering a more competitive and efficient biosimilar market are gaining momentum in many jurisdictions. These initiatives include:

“On the regulatory front, the FDA should provide timely leadership and clear rules that respond to evolving evidence about biosimilars.”[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7255927/)

These policy changes, if implemented effectively, could substantially improve the climate for biosimilar development and adoption. Harmonization of requirements across major markets would be particularly beneficial, allowing developers to pursue more efficient global development programs.

## Educational Initiatives for Healthcare Stakeholders

Addressing knowledge gaps among healthcare professionals and patients represents a critical priority for improving biosimilar adoption. Comprehensive educational initiatives are emerging from various sources:

Research published in 2024 emphasizes the importance of these educational efforts: “Understanding the interconnectedness of the barriers and enablers affecting the uptake of biosimilar medicines is an important concept… Systemic enablers in the form of policies appear to be the most effective overall levers in affecting uptake of biosimilars, with policy makers advised to give careful consideration to appropriately educating HCPs and positively framing biosimilars for patients.”[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11247062/)

As knowledge and comfort with biosimilars increase among all stakeholders, adoption barriers are likely to diminish, accelerating market penetration and cost savings.

## Technological Advances in Biosimilar Development

Technological innovations are transforming biosimilar development and manufacturing, potentially addressing many current challenges:

These technological advances are making biosimilar development and manufacturing more efficient and cost-effective while maintaining or enhancing quality standards. As these technologies mature and gain broader adoption, they will likely reduce development timelines and costs, allowing more companies to enter the biosimilar market and increasing competition.

## Key Takeaways

The journey of biosimilars from concept to market success involves navigating a complex landscape of challenges across regulatory, manufacturing, market access, legal, and commercial domains. The key insights from this analysis include:

Despite these challenges, the biosimilar market continues to grow, driven by the increasing need for cost-effective alternatives to expensive biologics. As policy reforms, educational initiatives, and technological advances address current barriers, biosimilars are likely to play an increasingly important role in expanding patient access to life-changing biologic therapies while contributing to healthcare system sustainability.

Abstract
--------

U.S. laws enacted since 1983 have aimed to enhance the development and marketing of new pharmaceutical products. We thoroughly characterized all new molecular entities, therapeutic biologics, and gene and cell therapies approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) during the period 1980–2022 in the context of these laws and regulations. Throughout the study period, the FDA approved 1355 new pharmaceutical products. The median FDA review time decreased from 26.6 months prior to the Prescription Drug User Fee Act (1992), which authorized the FDA to collect fees from drug companies to 9.9 months after the Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (2012), which created new designations that eliminated the requirement for evidence of added therapeutic benefit for FDA expedited drug review. The greatest increase in approvals occurred in antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs, biologics, and orphan drugs. More than half of new drug approvals benefited from regulatory designations and pathways that did not require addressing unmet medical needs or demonstrating therapeutic benefit over available alternatives. The legislative goal of bringing more drugs to the market faster has been achieved. Further studies are needed to determine the therapeutic value to patients of new drugs approved using expedited approval pathways.

Introduction
------------

The mission of the FDA includes protecting and promoting public health by ensuring the safety and efficacy of drugs and biological products. The FDA is also responsible for advancing public health by “helping to speed innovation”[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR1 "Food and Drug Administration (FDA). What we do; 
                  https://www.fda.gov/about-fda/what-we-do
                  
                 (2021)."). The speed of drug development depends partly on acquiring the evidence required for marketing approval regarding the benefits and harms in specific patient populations.

The FDA regulatory review and approval processes reflect U.S. laws and FDA regulations, which have aimed to decrease FDA review time and increase the number of marketed new drugs (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab1)). The Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) of 1992 authorized the FDA to collect fees from sponsor companies to expedite the review of drugs,representing a major milestone in FDA’s regulatory process with downstream effects on approval decisions. The FDA Safety and Innovation Act (FDASIA) of 2012 amended the fast-track designation and the accelerated approval pathway and removed the requirement of evidence of added therapeutic benefit over existing treatments. In addition, FDASIA created the breakthrough therapy designation, the rare pediatric disease priority review voucher, and the qualified infectious disease product designation (QIDP) for anti-infectives to treat serious or life-threatening diseases due to resistant or potentially resistant bacteria.

Previous studies have examined new drugs approved by the FDA during designated time frames[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR2 "Outterson, K., Powers, J. H., Seoane, E., Rodriguez-Monguio, R. & Kesselheim, A. Approval and withdrawal of new antibiotics and other antiinfectives in the U.S., 1980–2009. J. Law Med. Ethics 41, 688–696 (2013)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR3 "Kesselheim, A. S., Wang, B., Franklin, J. M. & Darrow, J. J. Trends in utilization of FDA expedited drug development and approval programs, 1987–2014: Cohort study. BMJ 351, h4633 (2015)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR4 "Hwang, T. J., Darrow, J. J. & Kesselheim, A. S. The FDA’s expedited programs and clinical development times for novel therapeutics, 2012–2016. JAMA 318, 2137–2138 (2017)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR5 "Darrow, J. J., Avorn, J. & Kesselheim, A. S. FDA approval and regulation of pharmaceuticals, 1983–2018. JAMA 323, 164–176 (2020)."),[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR6 "Darrow, J. J., Najafzadeh, M., Stefanini, K. & Kesselheim, A. S. Regulatory approval characteristics of antimicrobial versus non-antimicrobial products, 1984–2018: An evaluation of Food and Drug Administration flexibilities. Lancet Infect. Dis. 20, e159–e164 (2020)."). However, there is a lack of up to date, comprehensive studies that assess the characteristics of all FDA-approved new drugs and biologics across therapeutic classes within the context of major regulations implemented in the US since 1980. Given the substantial public and private resources invested in the development of new therapies and the dynamic regulatory environment, it is crucial to assess the extent to which these laws and FDA regulatory actions have effectively accomplished their intended objective of advancing public health through drug approvals with evidence of addressing unmet patient needs and improving patient outcomes[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR7 "Porter, M. E. What is value in health care?. N. Engl. J. Med. 363, 2477–2481 (2010)."). Therefore, we conducted a comprehensive assessment and characterization of all new molecular entities, therapeutic biologics, and gene and cell therapies approved by the FDA since 1980. Additionally, we analyzed the approval pathways and regulatory designations within the context of the legislative and regulatory landscape in the US.

Methods
-------

We collected the FDA regulatory information for all new active ingredients approved by the FDA for marketing in the U.S., including new molecular entities (NME)—a new drug never approved before by the FDA or marketed in the U.S.-, new therapeutic biologics, and gene and cell therapies in the period 1980 through 2022. We collected the application number, non-proprietary and brand names, FDA submission and approval dates, and the FDA regulatory designations and approval pathways. We extracted data for NMEs and therapeutic biologics from the Drugs@FDA, the Orange Book, and the Purple Book[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR18 "Food and Drug Administration (FDA). New Drugs at FDA: CDER's New Molecular Entities and New Therapeutic Biological Products; 
                  https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/daf
                  
                 (2023)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR19 "Approved Drug Products with Therapeutic Equivalence Evaluations—Orange Book-. 
                  https://www.fda.gov/drugs/drug-approvals-and-databases/approved-drug-products-therapeutic-equivalence-evaluations-orange-book
                  
                 (2023)."),[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR20 "Purple Book. Database of Licensed Biological Products; 
                  https://purplebooksearch.fda.gov
                  
                 (2023)."). We extracted gene and cell therapies data from the list of Approved Cellular and Gene Therapy Products approved in the US available at the Centers for Biologic Evaluation and Research[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR21 "Approved Cellular and Gene Therapy Products. 
                  https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/approved-cellular-and-gene-therapy-products
                  
                 (2023)."). We also collected information about notices of FDA regulatory actions available at the Federal Register[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR22 "Federal Register. 
                  https://www.federalregister.gov
                  
                 (2023).") and the therapeutic class from the WHO Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical Classification System[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR23 "World Health Organization Collaborating Center for Drug Statistics Methodology Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) Classification System. 
                  https://www.whocc.no/atc_ddd_index
                  
                 (2023).") (Supplemental Table [S1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#MOESM1)). We classified the drugs using the anatomical main group. We chose the therapeutic subgroup for systemic anti-infectives due to Congress approval of legislation incentivizing antibiotics and for diagnostic drugs because of their distinctions from products intended for treatment. We extracted all data through December 31, 2022. To ensure the reliability of the data, one investigator [ESV] was responsible for primary data extraction and placement into evidence tables and a second investigator [RRM] verified the data extraction and entry process. Data discrepancies were discussed and resolved by consensus.

The unit of analysis was the first application for each drug product. We calculated the FDA review time as the difference between the first new drug application (NDA) or biologic license application (BLA) submission and approval dates stated in FDA approval letters. We presented results by year of approval and three distinct periods defined by PDUFA (1992) and FDASIA (2012) enactment. Hence, the analysis was broken down by FDA approvals from 1980 to PDUFA enactment (1980-PDUFA), PDUFA to FDASIA enactment (PDUFA-FDASIA), and thereafter (FDASIA-2022).

Results
-------

### FDA approved new molecular entities, biologics and gene and cell therapy

In the period 1980–2022, the FDA approved 1355 new drugs, with an annual drug approval average ± standard deviation of 31.5 ± 12.0 drugs (Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab2)). The annual average number of approvals increased from 23.1 ± 6.1 (1980-PDUFA) to 29.8 ± 15.6 (PDUFA-FDASIA) and 45.0 ± 11.9 (FDASIA-2022) (Fig.[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Fig1)). FDA approvals included 1103 (81.4%) NME, 235 (17.3%) therapeutic biologics, and 17 (1.3%) gene and cell therapies. The annual average number of approved NME increased from 21.8 ± 6.4 (1980-PDUFA) to 24.7 ± 15.3 (PDUFA-FDASIA) and 32.1 ± 9.7 in FDASIA-2022. Whereas the annual average number of approved therapeutic biologics increased from 1.2 ± 1.0 (1980-PDUFA) to 5.0 ± 1.8 (PDUFA-FDASIA) and 11.5 ± 4.4 in FDASIA-2022. The FDA approved 17 gene and cell therapies in the period 2010 through 2022.

In the study period, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents (306, 22.6% of total approvals) had the greatest number of approvals among all therapeutic classes (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab1)). Cardiovascular system drugs (54, 18.2%) had the greatest number of approvals in the period from 1980 to the enactment of PDUFA. Since 1992, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents continued to dominate with the greatest number of approvals, both during the PDUFA-FDASIA period (111, 18.9%) and the FDASIA-2022 period (169, 35.8%) (Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab2)).

### FDA designations and expedited approval pathways

The FDA approved over half of the new drugs 788 (58.2%) using at least one designation or expedited approval pathway during the study period (Table [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab3)) with an annual average of 18.3 ± 10.7 new drugs. The annual average number of drugs approved through an FDA designation or expedited review increased from 11.3 ± 4.0 (145, 49.0%) in the period from 1980 to the enactment of PDUFA, to 14.8 ± 7.4 (292, 49.7%) in the PDUFA-FDASIA period, and 33.5 ± 10.4 (351, 74.4%) in the FDASIA-2022 period.

**Table 3 FDA designations and regulatory approval pathways for new drugs, 1980–2022.**

[Full size table](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7/tables/3)

The FDA granted priority review designation to 695 (51.3%) drugs, including 15 (1.1% of total approvals) drugs approved using a priority review voucher and 19 (1.4%) drugs granted the Qualified Infectious Disease Product Designation (QIDP). Since 1992, the FDA approved 133 (11.4%) drugs through the accelerated approval pathway using surrogate endpoints. In addition, the FDA approved 221 (26.2%) drugs using the fast-track review designation and granted breakthrough therapy designation to 116 (24.7%) new drugs (Table [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab3)).

The FDA granted at least one orphan designation to 498 (36.8%) drugs during the study period, including 412 new drugs with at least one orphan designation at first approval. The average number of approvals with at least one orphan designation increased from 4.8 ± 3.0 (61, 20.6%) in the period from 1983 to the enactment of PDUFA, to 9.5 ± 5.2 (187, 31.9%) in the PDUFA-FDASIA period, and 23.8 ± 7.9 (250, 53.0%) in the FDASIA-2022 period (Fig.[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Fig2)).

Antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents was the therapeutic class with the greatest percentage of approvals overall and with orphan drug designations (181, 59.7%), priority review designations (224 73.2%), accelerated approvals (89, 30.1%), fast track designations (88, 34.9%), and breakthrough therapy designations (62, 36.7%) (Table [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab3)).

Since the enactment of the QIDP designation in 2012, 19 (76.0%) out of 25 of the eligible antibacterials and antifungals were approved by the FDA using this designation. Additionally, the Limited Population Approval Pathway (LPAD) for antibacterial and antifungal drugs was used for two drugs: pretomanid and amikacin liposome inhalation suspensions. The FDA approved pretomanid on August 14, 2019, for the treatment of drug-resistant tuberculosis, while amikacin liposome inhalation suspension (new formulation) received FDA approval on September 28, 2018, for refractory lung infections caused by Mycobacterium avium complex. Moreover, the FDA granted the Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (RMAT) designation to two cell therapies: allogeneic cultured keratinocytes and dermal fibroblasts in murine collagen-dsat, as well as allogeneic processed thymus tissue–agdc. One gene therapy, lisocabtagene maraleucel, also received the RMAT designation.

### FDA approval review time

In the period 1980 through 2022, the median (interquartile range -IQR) FDA review time for new therapeutics was 12.0 (16.3) months (Table [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Tab4)). Specifically, the median (IQR) review time for priority review drugs was 8.1 (9.0) months, whereas for standard review drugs, it was 18.2 (19.9) months. Over time, the median FDA review time significantly decreased from 26.6 (23.6) months in the pre-PDUFA period, to 12.9 (14.9) in the PDUDA-FDASIA period, and 9.9 (4.1) months in the FDASIA-2022 period (Fig.[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#Fig3)). The review time varied by therapeutic class (Supplemental Table [S2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#MOESM2)).

Discussion
----------

This study comprehensively characterizes all FDA-approved new molecular entities, therapeutic biologics, and gene and cell therapies by therapeutic class and regulatory approval pathway since the first publication of the electronic version of the Approved Drug Products with Therapeutic Equivalence Evaluations, commonly known as the Orange Book, in 1980. Consistent with previous ad-hoc studies[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR3 "Kesselheim, A. S., Wang, B., Franklin, J. M. & Darrow, J. J. Trends in utilization of FDA expedited drug development and approval programs, 1987–2014: Cohort study. BMJ 351, h4633 (2015)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR4 "Hwang, T. J., Darrow, J. J. & Kesselheim, A. S. The FDA’s expedited programs and clinical development times for novel therapeutics, 2012–2016. JAMA 318, 2137–2138 (2017)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR5 "Darrow, J. J., Avorn, J. & Kesselheim, A. S. FDA approval and regulation of pharmaceuticals, 1983–2018. JAMA 323, 164–176 (2020)."),[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR6 "Darrow, J. J., Najafzadeh, M., Stefanini, K. & Kesselheim, A. S. Regulatory approval characteristics of antimicrobial versus non-antimicrobial products, 1984–2018: An evaluation of Food and Drug Administration flexibilities. Lancet Infect. Dis. 20, e159–e164 (2020)."),[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR24 "Sacks, L. V. et al. Scientific and regulatory reasons for delay and denial of FDA approval of initial applications for new drugs, 2000–2012. JAMA 311, 378–384 (2014)."), our study provides evidence that legislation enacted by U.S. Congress and FDA regulations implemented over the past four decades have effectively contributed to the increased number of pharmaceutical products approved for marketing in the US. Specifically, we found that over half of the pharmaceutical products obtained FDA approval using at least one FDA designation, particularly the orphan drug designation, or expedited approval pathway. Since the enactment of PDUFA in 1992, the average number of approvals using the priority review designation more than doubled, and more than one in ten of the new drug approvals used accelerated approval pathways based on a surrogate endpoint. Since 1997, over one-fourth of the new drug approvals have been approved using the fast-track designation, where no evidence demonstrating the potential to address an unmet medical need is required. Lastly, since the enactment of FDASIA in 2012, more than one in five new drug approvals used the breakthrough therapy designation based on preliminary clinical evidence of potential improvement over available therapies. This increase in approvals was primarily driven by therapeutic biologics with a notable emphasis on those indicated for cancer conditions.

We also found a significant reduction in the FDA regulatory review time after the enactment of PDUFA in 1992, a finding that is consistent with a previous study[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR5 "Darrow, J. J., Avorn, J. & Kesselheim, A. S. FDA approval and regulation of pharmaceuticals, 1983–2018. JAMA 323, 164–176 (2020)."). A study reviewing drug approval applications in the period 2000–2011 concluded that the FDA completed 90% of the priority reviews within the 6 months of PDUFA established timeframe[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR25 "Government Accountability Office. FDA has met most performance goals for reviewing applications; 
                  https://www.gao.gov/assets/590/589762.pdf
                  
                 (2012)"). We found a reduction in the median review time from 27.1 months before PDUFA to 9.9 months after FDASIA. Greater reductions in the FDA review time could have been achieved if preventable sponsor deficiencies during the drug development process (populations, endpoints, adverse events, optimal doses, and labeling errors) had not occurred[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR25 "Government Accountability Office. FDA has met most performance goals for reviewing applications; 
                  https://www.gao.gov/assets/590/589762.pdf
                  
                 (2012)").

In the late 1980s, insufficient federal funding adversely impacted FDA operations, contributing to a backlog of drug applications [26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-53554-7#ref-CR26 "Woodcock J, Junod S. Launching. PDUFA Lays the Foundation: Launching into the Era of User Fee Acts U.S. Food and Drug Administration; 
                  https://www.fda.gov/media/110364/download
                  
                ."). PDUFA provided the FDA with additional resources to expedite new drug application reviews, resulting in a significant reduction in FDA review times. The acceptance of industry fees has raised concerns about the independence and transparency of FDA when regu
